ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cashew nut entry; the roasted and salted preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cashew nuts as a commodity; roasted and salted preparation is not separately coded in FoodEx2. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227493005 covers cashew nuts generically; roasted salted variant not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted salted cashews is approximately 30g (a small handful), providing around 174 kcal, 5.1g protein, 14.3g fat, and 9.0g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted cashew nut, salted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for roasted cashew nut, salted (cr_id F09030003). All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this source with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 22 for cashew nuts is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283), which report GI ~22 for cashew nuts. Glycemic load per 30g serving calculated as: (22 × 9.05g available carbohydrate per 30g serving) / 100 = 2.0 (rounded). Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate minus dietary fibre = (30.16 − 3.3) × 0.30 = 8.06g net carbs per 30g serving; GL = (22 × 8.06) / 100 ≈ 1.8, rounded to 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 308mg/100g from added salt, partially offset by high potassium (565mg/100g) and dietary fibre (3.3g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 30g per serving (approximately 92mg sodium) and consider choosing unsalted cashews if hypertension is poorly controlled, as the potassium-to-sodium ratio remains favourable for blood pressure management at controlled portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~22), high protein (16.84g/100g), dietary fibre (3.3g/100g), and healthy unsaturated fats supporting insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted salted cashews are suitable for Type 2 diabetes management in 30g controlled portions due to their low GI (~22), high protein, and fibre content, but the relatively higher carbohydrate content (30.2g/100g) compared to other nuts warrants careful portion monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High unsaturated fat content (MUFA ~27.3g + PUFA ~7.8g per 100g) with moderate saturated fat (9.5g/100g), zero cholesterol, and beneficial potassium and fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cashews provide a cardioprotective unsaturated fat profile (MUFA + PUFA ~35g/100g) with zero cholesterol; limit to 30g portions to manage caloric density (581 kcal/100g) and choose unsalted varieties to minimise sodium intake for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: MUFA/PUFA dominant fat profile (~35g/100g unsaturated fat), anti-inflammatory minerals (magnesium, zinc, iron 6.05mg/100g), and dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cashews qualify for the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out as whole nuts and are suitable for RA management in 30g portions; choose unsalted cashews to avoid the pro-inflammatory effect of added sodium (308mg/100g) on RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted salted cashews (Anacardium occidentale) are kidney-shaped tree nuts that have been dry-roasted and seasoned with salt, producing a crunchy texture and rich, buttery-savoury flavour. They are widely consumed as a snack food across Singapore, Southeast Asia, South Asia, and globally. Nutritionally, per 100g they provide 581 kcal, 16.84g protein, 47.77g total fat (predominantly monounsaturated ~27.3g and polyunsaturated ~7.8g, with saturated fat ~9.5g), 30.16g carbohydrate, 5.01g sugar, 3.3g dietary fibre, and 308mg sodium from added salt. They are rich in potassium (565mg/100g), calcium (43mg/100g), and iron (6.05mg/100g). The glycemic index is low (~22), making them suitable for blood glucose management in controlled portions. Clinically, cashews are beneficial for cardiovascular health due to their unsaturated fat dominance and zero cholesterol; suitable for Type 2 diabetes management due to low GI and high protein and fibre; and moderately cautioned for hypertension due to added salt content, though the high potassium-to-sodium ratio is favourable. They are vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. A standard serving is 30g (a small handful), providing approximately 174 kcal.
common_user_queries: Can I eat cashews if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in roasted salted cashews?
What is the GI of cashews?
How much sodium in salted cashews?
Are cashews good for heart health?
What is a healthy portion of cashews?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F09030003) with confidence 0.95. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, potassium, and cholesterol values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (NDB 12087, cashew nuts, dry roasted, with salt added) for consistency. All values are internally consistent and plausible.
notes: Saturated fat (9.5g/100g), monounsaturated fat (27.3g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (7.8g/100g), potassium (565mg/100g), and cholesterol (0.0mg/100g) values are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cashew nuts, dry roasted, with salt added (NDB 12087), as the HPB source does not separately report fatty acid fractions or potassium. Trans fat is 0.0g (confirmed negligible for dry-roasted tree nuts per USDA). Vitamin B12 is 0.0g (naturally zero — plant-based food contains no vitamin B12). Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg (naturally zero — cashews contain negligible vitamin D). Fields set to -1 (data not available in either HPB or USDA for this specific preparation): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data not separately reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific roasted salted preparation; USDA FoodData Central values exist for the generic category but were not used to avoid source mixing without explicit adaptation documentation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 22 × net carbohydrate per 30g serving 8.06g) / 100 ≈ 1.8, rounded to 2.0. The input record listed condition key 'diabetes' which has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Ontology codes are approximate matches as no dedicated FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT code exists for the roasted-and-salted preparation variant specifically.
